---
title: "AI Tools for Researchers: A Practical Introduction"
author: ~
date: '2026-03-04'
slug: ai-tools-for-researchers
categories: []
tags: [ai, copilot, quarto, tools]
images: []
---

> **RESQUANT seminar resource.** This post is designed to be a clickable companion for a live demo session. Work through it at your own pace, or use it afterwards to set things up yourself.

---

# What are we doing today?

This session covers three things:

1. **What AI tools actually are** — enough to use them confidently and critically
2. **Microsoft Copilot** — the AI assistant you already have access to via your university Microsoft 365 account
3. **GitHub Copilot in RStudio** — AI-assisted coding, and how to set it up
4. **Quarto** — the modern evolution of R Markdown, and why it's worth switching

---

# Part 1: What is AI? 

## The short version

The AI tools we're talking about — ChatGPT, Copilot, Claude — are **large language models (LLMs)**. They are trained on enormous amounts of text and learn to predict what comes next in a sequence of words. That's it. That's the core trick. But it turns out that doing this very well produces something that can write, summarise, reason, code, and explain.

They are not:

- Searching the internet (unless explicitly told to)
- "Thinking" in the way humans do
- Infallible — they can and do produce convincing nonsense ("hallucinations")

They *are* very good at:

- Drafting and editing text
- Explaining concepts
- Writing and debugging code
- Summarising documents
- Generating structured output (tables, lists, templates)

## Resources 

**The University of Manchester** has its own [AI Hub guidelines](https://www.staffnet.manchester.ac.uk/ai-hub/guidelines/covering how staff should use AI tools in teaching, research, and everyday work. 

The key principles are:  
- transparency (declare how you've used AI), 
- accountability (you own the output — verify it), 
- competence (know how to use AI well) 
- responsible use (ethical, legal, fair), 
- respect (society, individual rights, environment)

For anything involving sensitive data, use the university's **enterprise Microsoft Copilot** rather than public tools like ChatGPT — it keeps your data within university systems.

Some research-specific issues raised:      
- synthesised data must be clearly labelled as such, 
- corresponding author responsible for all written content, so check anything that you use AI for
- reviewers should never upload confidential manuscript content to public AI services. 

Worth a read before you start integrating these tools into your workflow.


### External stuff

- **AI Snake Oil** A book and project by academics (Prof and PhD student at Princeton) who are cautious about AI but not just doom and gloom. They have a newsletter [https://www.normaltech.ai/](https://www.normaltech.ai/) and a book we have in the library [https://www.librarysearch.manchester.ac.uk/permalink/44MAN_INST/1l23p04/alma992988705349101631](https://www.librarysearch.manchester.ac.uk/permalink/44MAN_INST/1l23p04/alma992988705349101631) and a talk [https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=C3TqcUEFR58](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=C3TqcUEFR58) 
- **Anthropic** has some useful videos in their AI Claude here: [https://www.anthropic.com/learn](https://www.anthropic.com/learn)
- Andy Wheeler has a book out I just ordered for the library: [https://crimede-coder.com/blogposts/2026/LLMsForMortals](https://crimede-coder.com/blogposts/2026/LLMsForMortals) - the examples are in python but I suspect easy to adapt to R

## A note on limitations for research

- **Don't trust AI for citations or facts** — always verify
- **Data privacy matters** — don't paste sensitive or confidential data into public AI tools
- 


---

# Part 2: Microsoft Copilot

## What is it?

Microsoft Copilot is Microsoft's AI assistant, built on OpenAI's GPT models. If your institution has Microsoft 365, you likely have access to Copilot already — it's built into Word, Outlook, Teams, and available as a standalone chat interface.

**Access it here:** [copilot.microsoft.com](https://copilot.microsoft.com) — sign in with your university Microsoft account.

## What can you use it for?

**In Word:**

- Drafting sections from bullet points
- Summarising long documents
- Rewriting for tone or audience
- Generating tables and structured content

**In Outlook:**

- Drafting email replies
- Summarising long email threads

**In Teams:**

- Summarising meeting transcripts
- Generating action points from meetings

**As a standalone chat:**

- Much like ChatGPT: general purpose, good for thinking out loud, drafting, explaining concepts

## Practical demo prompts to try

```
Summarise the following abstract in two sentences for a non-specialist audience: [paste abstract]
```

```
I'm writing a methods section for a quantitative criminology paper.
Draft a paragraph explaining [your method] for a general social science audience.
```

```
Here are my bullet points for a discussion section.
Turn these into coherent prose, academic in tone: [paste bullets]
```

## Tips

- **Be specific**: the more context you give, the better the output
- **Iterate**: treat it as a first draft, not a final product
- **Use it for structure**: great for getting past blank page paralysis

---

# Part 3: GitHub Copilot in RStudio

## What is GitHub Copilot?

[GitHub Copilot](https://github.com/features/copilot) is an AI coding assistant that suggests code as you type, directly inside your editor. It's trained on public code repositories and is particularly good at:

- Completing functions you've started
- Suggesting the next logical step in a data pipeline
- Writing boilerplate code (loading packages, reading data, etc.)
- Explaining what a piece of code does

## Getting access

GitHub Copilot is a paid service, but:

- **Free for verified students and educators** via the [GitHub Education programme](https://education.github.com/)
- If you have an academic email address, you can likely get access for free

Steps:

1. Create a [GitHub account](https://github.com) if you don't have one
2. Apply for [GitHub Education](https://education.github.com/benefits) using your university email — select "Teacher" or "Researcher" as appropriate
3. Once approved, Copilot is available under your account settings

## Setting up in RStudio

RStudio (version 2023.09.0 or later) has native GitHub Copilot integration.

**Steps:**

1. Open RStudio → `Tools` → `Global Options` → `Copilot`
2. Click `Sign In` and follow the prompts to authenticate with your GitHub account
3. Enable Copilot suggestions

Once enabled, suggestions appear as greyed-out text as you type. Press `Tab` to accept.

**Helpful links:**

- [Posit's GitHub Copilot documentation](https://docs.posit.co/ide/user/ide/guide/tools/copilot.html)
- [GitHub Copilot documentation](https://docs.github.com/en/copilot)

## Using Copilot effectively in R

Copilot works best when you give it context. The most reliable way to do this is with a **comment describing what you want**:

```{r}
# Load the tidyverse and read in a CSV called "crime_data.csv" from the data folder

# Create a bar chart showing counts of crime type, ordered from most to least frequent

# Filter to include only incidents in 2023, then group by month and summarise total count

```

The more descriptive your comment, the better the suggestion. Think of it as pair programming with someone who types faster than you.

## Live demo

- Open a new R script
- Write a comment and watch Copilot suggest the code
- Accept with `Tab`, cycle through alternatives with `Alt+]`

---

# Part 4: Quarto

## What is Quarto?

[Quarto](https://quarto.org/) is the successor to R Markdown, developed by Posit (the company behind RStudio). If you've used R Markdown, it will feel immediately familiar — but it works across R, Python, and Julia, and produces much more polished output by default.

Key things Quarto can produce:

- HTML reports and websites
- PDF documents
- Word documents
- Presentations (via reveal.js)
- Academic manuscripts (including journal submission formats)
- Entire books

## Why switch from R Markdown?

- **Better defaults**: output looks nicer out of the box
- **More output formats**: especially good for slides and websites
- **Cross-language**: works with Python and Julia too, not just R
- **Active development**: Quarto likely to replace rmarkdown?
- **Easier to learn**: am told it's even easier to pick up.


## Getting started

AFAIK quarto is now included with your RStudio download, so you just start a new qmd document and go from there. 

It basically looks like Rmarkdown, but you can use the visual editor too


## Key resources

- [Quarto documentation](https://quarto.org/docs/guide/) — comprehensive and well-written
- [R for Data Science (2nd ed.)](https://r4ds.hadley.nz/quarto.html) — chapter on Quarto by Hadley Wickham
- [Quarto gallery](https://quarto.org/docs/gallery/) — examples of what's possible
- [Awesome Quarto](https://github.com/mcanouil/awesome-quarto) — curated list of Quarto resources, extensions, and examples
- [Quarto books](https://blog.stephenturner.us/p/quarto-books) by Stephen Turner: moving from bookdown/ rmarkdown to quarto, with lots of good resource links in there. 

---

# Putting it all together

These tools work well in combination:

| Task | Tool |
|------|------|
| Understanding a method or concept | Microsoft Copilot / ChatGPT |
| Writing and editing prose | Microsoft Copilot (in Word) |
| Writing R code | GitHub Copilot (in RStudio) |
| Producing reports / papers | Quarto |
| Reproducible research output | Quarto + GitHub |
